---
title: "Predicting Churn & Billing"
subtitle: "Telco Customer Churn dataset — end-to-end ML"
author: "Matej"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
---

## The problem

- 7,043 telecom customers
- Two real business questions:
  1. **Will a customer churn** (cancel)?
  2. **What should their monthly bill be**, given their plan?

::: {.notes}
Framing for a non-technical audience: this is about keeping customers and billing them
correctly.
:::

## Why it matters

- Losing a customer costs far more than keeping one
- Billing errors erode trust and cost revenue either direction
- Both are answerable from data the company already has

## Our approach

1. Clean the data (fix a broken numeric column, handle new-customer edge cases)
2. Explore it — find the patterns *before* trusting any model
3. Engineer a handful of simple, explainable features
4. Build a ladder of models — simple to advanced — for each question
5. Compare every model honestly against a "do-nothing" baseline
6. Ship a live app that makes real predictions

## What we found — Churn

- ~26.5% of customers churn
- **Month-to-month contracts churn far more** than 1-2 year contracts
- **Churn is concentrated in new customers** (first few months)
- Our best model (Logistic Regression) meaningfully beats
  "just guess nobody churns"

## What we found — Billing

- Monthly bill is almost entirely explained by **plan + add-ons**
- Same plan → nearly the same bill (~$1 variation)
- A simple model predicts bills very accurately — useful as a
  **pricing sanity check**, not a way to set prices

## Honest limits

- Churn model: real signal, not perfect — a decision-support tool, not
  an autopilot
- Billing model: high accuracy because pricing is systematic here, not
  because the model found something exotic
- Neither model should make automated decisions without a human in the loop

## The app

- **Churn Predictor** — enter a customer's profile, get a churn probability
- **Charges Predictor** — enter a plan, get an expected bill
- **Model Leaderboard** — see exactly how every model performed, baseline included

## Recommendations

1. Focus retention efforts on new, month-to-month customers
2. Use the churn model to triage, not to decide
3. Use the billing model to catch outlier accounts, not to price
4. Keep reporting honestly — a model close to baseline is a *finding*, not a failure
